findings.description: Generated HTML declares no <meta charset="utf-8"> even though save_report writes UTF-8, so non-ASCII display names can render garbled in browsers that default to another encoding.
The border="1" attribute is obsolete HTML; styling belongs in CSS.
ReportGenerator and most of its public methods lack docstrings (only fetch_events documents its contract).
A new SQLite connection is opened and torn down on every method call with no reuse, adding per-call overhead under frequent invocation.
No validation that start <= end, so an inverted range silently produces an empty report instead of raising an error.
fetch_events returns raw sqlite3.Row objects while top_users returns plain dicts, leaking the persistence type and making the public API inconsistent.

The predicate created_at < end.isoformat() excludes every event on the end day, contradicting the docstring's promise that both boundary days of [start, end] are included (needs end + 1 day or <=-style bound).

Date filtering relies on lexicographic string comparison and assumes created_at is stored as ISO-8601 text; epoch integers or other formats would silently match nothing or match incorrectly under SQLite's cross-type ordering.
fetchall() materializes the entire date range in memory with no LIMIT or streaming, so a wide range over a large events table can exhaust memory.
Building rows via repeated string += in a loop is potentially quadratic; ''.join over a list (or a generator) is the idiomatic linear approach.
display_name, action, and created_at are interpolated into HTML without html.escape, enabling stored XSS against admins viewing the report via a malicious user display name or action string.
The caller-supplied title is substituted into <title> and <h1> unescaped, allowing HTML/script injection whenever the title derives from user input.
The unsanitized title is written to the log, so embedded newlines could forge or split log entries (log injection).
save_report writes to an unvalidated out_path, permitting arbitrary file overwrite or path traversal if the path is ever attacker-influenced.
top_users uses the same created_at < end bound, so activity on the end day is excluded from the ranking, inconsistent with the inclusive-range contract documented for fetch_events.
limit is not validated; in SQLite a negative LIMIT means unlimited, so limit=-1 would return every user and limit=0 returns nothing without error.
The two event_count branches duplicate the base query text and parameter tuple, which could be shared to avoid drift between branches.
event_count (both branches, lines 82 and 88) also uses created_at < end, undercounting by excluding all events on the end day relative to the documented inclusive-range intent.
